heroOverview: Macrocheilia (ICD-10: Q18.6) is a congenital condition in which one or both lips are abnormally large due to excessive tissue volume — most commonly excess connective tissue, lymphatic tissue, or both — present from birth or becoming apparent in early infancy. The upper lip, lower lip, or both may be affected, producing fullness, eversion, or protrusion of lip tissue that is disproportionate to the rest of the face. Macrocheilia may be isolated or a manifestation of broader conditions involving lymphatic or vascular overgrowth, such as lymphangioma, Melkersson-Rosenthal syndrome, or double lip deformity. Lip enlargement in congenital macrocheilia differs mechanically from inflammatory causes such as angioedema, in that it is persistent rather than episodic. Depending on severity, macrocheilia affects feeding, lip seal, speech, oral hygiene, and psychosocial wellbeing. Treatment ranges from conservative management of mild cases to surgical lip reduction (cheiloplasty) in those with significant functional or cosmetic impairment.

keyStats.prevalence: No precise population-level incidence figure exists; macrocheilia is rare in its isolated congenital form and far more common as part of a lymphatic or syndromic condition
keyStats.demographics: Affects males and females equally when isolated; Melkersson-Rosenthal syndrome shows a slight female predominance
keyStats.avgAge: Congenital macrocheilia is apparent at birth; Melkersson-Rosenthal onset is typically in the second or third decade of life
keyStats.globalCases: Very rare in isolation; lymphangiomatous macrocheilia accounts for a larger proportion of reported paediatric cases

definition: Macrocheilia (ICD-10: Q18.6) describes an abnormal increase in the volume of one or both lips, classifiable under congenital malformations of face and neck (Q10–Q18). In its congenital form, the excessive lip volume reflects either developmental overgrowth of connective tissue stroma, abnormal accumulation of lymphatic channels (lymphangioma of the lip), or a combination of both. True congenital macrocheilia may occur as an isolated anomaly or as a feature of oculoauriculovertebral spectrum, double lip deformity, Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome (in which macroglossia and somatic overgrowth accompany lip enlargement), or vascular malformation syndromes. Melkersson-Rosenthal syndrome — an uncommon neurocutaneous condition characterised by recurrent facial swelling, scrotal tongue, and facial palsy — produces a granulomatous macrocheilia that represents the non-congenital end of the spectrum and is included here because clinical features overlap. In lymphangiomatous macrocheilia, microscopy shows dilated lymphatic channels filled with protein-rich fluid in the submucosa; in granulomatous macrocheilia, non-caseating granulomas are the histological hallmark. Functional consequences include impaired lip closure, drooling, difficulty forming bilabial phonemes, and — particularly in the lower lip — chronic trauma from occlusion or biting.

typesClassification.description: Enlargement of one or both lips from excess connective tissue or embryonic overgrowth without an associated syndrome or systemic disease; the rarest form.
Lip enlargement due to congenital lymphatic channel malformation (lymphangioma) in the lip submucosa; typically present from birth and may enlarge gradually in the first years of life.

A fold of excess mucosa on the inner surface of the upper lip produces an apparent doubling of the lip when the mouth is open or smiling; often congenital but may be accentuated during puberty with dental development.
Persistent lip swelling caused by non-caseating granulomatous inflammation; part of a triad with scrotal tongue and recurrent facial palsy; onset usually in adolescence or early adulthood.

primarySymptoms: Visible enlargement of one or both lips that is disproportionate to facial dimensions, often noted by parents immediately after birth.
Lip protrusion or eversion, where the mucosal surface of the enlarged lip faces outward rather than being tucked normally behind the teeth.
Drooling secondary to impaired lip seal; saliva pools behind the protuberant lip and escapes at rest.
Difficulty with breastfeeding and bottle feeding in infants because a bulky lip interferes with the oral seal needed for suction.
Articulation difficulty with bilabial sounds (b, p, m) when lip closure requires overcoming the additional tissue bulk.
Trauma to the enlarged lower lip from biting or from contact with the upper incisors during occlusion.
Chronic moisture and maceration of the lip mucosa, predisposing to angular cheilitis and fungal superinfection.
Cosmetic asymmetry of the lower face when macrocheilia is unilateral.
Social embarrassment and psychosocial distress in school-aged children and adolescents.
In granulomatous macrocheilia: episodic worsening during upper respiratory infections, with gradual residual enlargement between episodes over years.
earlyWarningSigns: Neonatal lip tissue that appears substantially fuller than expected on clinical examination, particularly if bilateral.
Visible vesicles or cysts on the lip surface in infants, suggesting lymphangiomatous involvement.
Recurring lip swelling in a teenager that does not fully resolve between episodes, suggesting early Melkersson-Rosenthal syndrome.
A soft, compressible fullness of the lip on palpation, consistent with lymphatic or connective tissue overgrowth rather than oedema.
emergencySigns: Sudden dramatic increase in lip volume with urticaria, throat tightening, or breathing difficulty — distinguishes anaphylaxis-related angioedema from macrocheilia and requires emergency treatment with epinephrine.
Airway compromise in a neonate with massive lip enlargement from a large lymphangioma extending beyond the lip.
Severe haemorrhage from a traumatised vascular malformation within the enlarged lip.

causes.description: Developmental failure of lymphatic channels to communicate with the venous system results in dilated, sequestered lymphatic spaces in the lip submucosa. These dilated channels distend the lip from birth and may enlarge slowly during childhood as lymph accumulates.

Isolated mesenchymal hyperplasia of the lip stroma without a specific lymphatic or vascular malformation produces a firm, diffuse enlargement; the cellular mechanism involves excess fibroblast activity during lip development in the first trimester.

Recurring non-caseating granuloma formation in the lip dermis and submucosa produces progressive lymphoedema and interstitial fibrosis; the trigger for granuloma formation is unknown but may involve dysregulated innate immunity directed at microbial antigens.

Venous or mixed venous-lymphatic malformations of the lip, present from birth, produce soft, compressible lip swelling that may enlarge with Valsalva manoeuvre or with hormonal changes at puberty; they are classified under vascular anomalies rather than true congenital malformations of lips.

Somatic overgrowth syndromes caused by IGF2 pathway dysregulation or chromosome 11p15 imprinting defects produce macroglossia, visceromegaly, and generalised tissue excess including enlarged lips; macrocheilia in this setting is part of a broader overgrowth phenotype.

A congenital fold of accessory mucosa on the inner upper lip — the double lip deformity — presents at birth as an apparent doubling of lip tissue; though architecturally different from true tissue hypertrophy, it is clinically classified under macrocheilia.

riskFactors.factor: Known vascular or lymphatic malformation syndrome
riskFactors.description: Children with Klippel-Trenaunay syndrome, CLOVES syndrome, or PIK3CA-related overgrowth spectrum involving the face have elevated risk of lymphatic or venous malformation of the lips.
Familial clustering of Melkersson-Rosenthal syndrome suggests a genetic contribution, though most cases appear sporadic; first-degree relatives of an affected person have modestly elevated risk.
Children with BWS present with generalised overgrowth including macroglossia and macrocheilia; the condition is diagnosed by characteristic features and methylation testing of chromosome 11p15.
Any interference with fetal lymphatic drainage — from extrinsic compression or intrinsic abnormality — can cause lymphatic vessel dilatation and tissue swelling in the lip and adjacent structures.
Some patients with granulomatous macrocheilia have concurrent atopy, inflammatory bowel disease, or sarcoidosis, suggesting a shared tendency toward granulomatous inflammation.

affectedDemographics: Neonates and infants with congenital lymphangiomatous or connective-tissue macrocheilia, identified at birth
Children and adolescents with Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome or vascular malformation syndromes
Adolescents and young adults developing Melkersson-Rosenthal syndrome, with slight female predominance
Patients with orofacial granulomatosis, a related granulomatous lip condition often seen in the second or third decade
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of macrocheilia is primarily clinical, based on direct inspection and palpation of the enlarged lip. The clinician assesses the consistency (soft and fluctuant in lymphangioma, firm in connective tissue overgrowth, rubbery in granulomatous disease), the distribution (focal versus diffuse), and whether the enlargement is static or progressive. A full history explores episodic swelling (Melkersson-Rosenthal), known syndromic diagnoses, and systemic symptoms of inflammatory bowel disease. In children with additional features suggesting a syndrome, chromosomal microarray and methylation studies for Beckwith-Wiedemann should be obtained. When the aetiology is not clear clinically, MRI of the lip and face with fat-saturation sequences is the investigation of choice: lymphatic malformations show characteristic T2-hyperintense multi-cystic spaces, vascular malformations enhance with contrast, and granulomatous disease shows diffuse enhancement without cystic spaces. Lip biopsy is performed when granulomatous macrocheilia is suspected, and histology demonstrating non-caseating granulomas confirms Melkersson-Rosenthal or orofacial granulomatosis. Angioedema must be excluded by history (episodic, resolves fully, associated urticaria or triggers) and, if hereditary angioedema is suspected, by measuring C1 esterase inhibitor levels and function.

diagnosticTests.test: MRI of face and lips with fat saturation
diagnosticTests.purpose: Characterises the internal architecture of enlarged lip tissue, distinguishing lymphatic malformation, vascular malformation, and granulomatous infiltration — the three commonest pathologies — to guide definitive treatment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 30–60 minute scan; infants and young children usually require sedation or general anaesthesia; intravenous contrast may be given.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Histological confirmation of granulomatous macrocheilia (non-caseating granulomas) or lymphangioma (dilated lymphatic channels) when the diagnosis is uncertain after imaging.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small piece of lip tissue is removed under local or general anaesthesia; the wound is closed with a suture and heals in 1–2 weeks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects syndromic causes of overgrowth (Beckwith-Wiedemann) or chromosomal imbalances associated with vascular malformation syndromes.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw with results in 2–4 weeks; methylation studies use the same sample.

diagnosticTests.test: C1 esterase inhibitor (C1-INH) level and function
diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes hereditary angioedema, which mimics macrocheilia with episodic severe lip swelling but is managed completely differently with C1-INH concentrate or icatibant.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw; results typically available within 3–5 working days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A quick first-line investigation to detect abnormal vascular flow within the enlarged lip, suggesting a vascular malformation, before committing to MRI.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A gel is applied to the lip and a probe placed lightly over the surface; entirely painless, taking 10–15 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates whether lip bulk causes feeding difficulty, aspiration risk, or caloric inadequacy requiring early intervention.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A speech-language pathologist or lactation consultant observes a full feed; non-invasive, takes 30–60 minutes.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of macrocheilia depends on its aetiology, severity, and functional impact. Mild isolated macrocheilia with no feeding, speech, or dental consequence may be observed through childhood and adolescence, as some lip bulk decreases with facial growth. When functional problems — including feeding difficulty, drooling, speech distortion, or chronic lip trauma — are present, or when cosmetic impact causes significant psychosocial distress, surgical lip reduction (cheiloplasty) is the primary intervention. Cheiloplasty reduces excess mucosal and submucosal tissue through intra-oral incisions, minimising visible scarring, and is performed under general anaesthesia. For lymphangiomatous macrocheilia, sclerotherapy with bleomycin, OK-432 (picibanil), or doxycycline is an effective first-line treatment that can reduce lesion volume by 50–80% and may obviate or reduce the extent of surgery. Granulomatous macrocheilia from Melkersson-Rosenthal syndrome is treated with intralesional triamcinolone, oral doxycycline, metronidazole, or — in refractory cases — systemic hydroxychloroquine or tumour necrosis factor inhibitors. In Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome, lip reduction is coordinated with macroglossia management. Post-surgical speech therapy and lip exercise programmes support functional recovery.

medicalTreatments.name: Sclerotherapy (lymphangioma)
medicalTreatments.description: Intralesional injection of sclerosing agents such as OK-432 (picibanil), bleomycin, or doxycycline into lymphangiomatous spaces causes sclerosis and collapse of the lymphatic channels, reducing lip volume over 4–8 weeks. Multiple sessions may be required.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: OK-432 achieves excellent or good response (greater than 50% volume reduction) in approximately 70–80% of macrocystic lymphatic malformations; microcystic lesions respond less reliably.

medicalTreatments.name: Intralesional triamcinolone (granulomatous macrocheilia)
medicalTreatments.description: Corticosteroid injected directly into the granulomatous lip tissue suppresses the granulomatous inflammatory response and can substantially reduce lip volume; typically repeated every 4–8 weeks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reported response rates of 50–70% in granulomatous macrocheilia; most effective in early-stage disease before fibrosis has established.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral doxycycline or metronidazole (Melkersson-Rosenthal)
medicalTreatments.description: Long courses of low-dose doxycycline or metronidazole reduce the granulomatous inflammatory load in orofacial granulomatosis and Melkersson-Rosenthal syndrome, achieving remission in a proportion of patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Approximately 40–60% of patients achieve reduction in lip swelling; systematic evidence is limited to case series.

medicalTreatments.name: Speech and language therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Post-surgical rehabilitation targets bilabial and labiodental phoneme production impaired by the enlarged lip; oral motor exercises improve lip seal and articulation in school-aged children.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Most children with isolated macrocheilia achieve age-appropriate articulation after cheiloplasty and targeted speech therapy.

surgicalOptions.name: Cheiloplasty (lip reduction surgery)
surgicalOptions.description: Resection of excess mucosal and submucosal tissue of one or both lips through intra-oral incisions, with careful reconstruction to produce a natural vermilion border and lip contour; performed under general anaesthesia.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Good to excellent cosmetic and functional results in over 85% of patients; revision surgery in up to 15% for residual volume or asymmetry.

surgicalOptions.name: Combined cheiloplasty and sclerotherapy
surgicalOptions.description: For lymphangiomatous macrocheilia, sclerotherapy reduces the lesion volume before surgery, minimising surgical extent and blood loss; surgery then removes the remaining collapsed lymphatic tissue and reshapes the lip.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Higher satisfaction rates than surgery alone in lymphangiomatous cases; complication rates reduced when sclerotherapy substantially pre-reduces the lesion.

alternativeTreatments.name: Sirolimus (mTOR inhibitor) for vascular malformations
alternativeTreatments.description: Oral sirolimus reduces the size of slow-flow vascular malformations including lymphatic components; it is used in refractory cases of lymphangiomatous macrocheilia where sclerotherapy and surgery have not controlled the condition.

alternativeTreatments.name: Laser therapy (for surface lymphangiomatous vesicles)
alternativeTreatments.description: Carbon dioxide or Nd:YAG laser ablates superficial lymphangiomatous vesicles on the lip surface, reducing vesicle leakage and improving the surface appearance without altering the deeper tissue bulk.

whySeeSpecialist: A craniofacial or maxillofacial surgeon should assess macrocheilia for surgical candidacy and technique selection. An interventional radiologist experienced in vascular anomalies should manage lymphangiomatous cases for sclerotherapy planning. When Melkersson-Rosenthal syndrome or orofacial granulomatosis is suspected, a dermatologist or oral medicine specialist familiar with granulomatous conditions is essential. For syndromic macrocheilia, a clinical geneticist coordinates the diagnosis and systemic surveillance.
doctorSelectionGuide: For surgical treatment, seek a paediatric or craniofacial plastic surgeon who performs cheiloplasty regularly and has specific experience with vascular anomalies of the lip. For lymphangiomatous macrocheilia, a specialist vascular anomalies centre with an interventional radiologist experienced in sclerotherapy offers the most comprehensive care. Ask specifically about the centre's experience with lip reduction and sclerotherapy volume and outcomes.
preventionStrategies: No specific prevention is established for isolated congenital macrocheilia.
Genetic counselling for families with a known vascular malformation syndrome or Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome before subsequent pregnancies.
Fetal MRI or detailed prenatal ultrasound when a syndromic diagnosis associated with lip overgrowth has been confirmed to allow postnatal management planning.
For Melkersson-Rosenthal syndrome, early treatment of granulomatous episodes before fibrosis establishes may reduce long-term lip volume.
lifestyleModifications: Protect the enlarged lip from biting trauma with a soft upper dental guard or bite plate if the lower lip is repeatedly traumatised during occlusion.
Apply a barrier cream or lanolin to the everted lip mucosa to prevent drying and cracking.
Maintain scrupulous oral hygiene with a small-headed brush, as excess lip tissue traps food debris at the gingival margin.
Report any rapid increase in lip volume or associated difficulty breathing to the treating team immediately.
For Melkersson-Rosenthal syndrome, identify and avoid any personal triggers for flares (certain foods, infections, stress) with the help of a food and symptom diary.

recommended: Soft, easily manipulated foods that do not require vigorous bilabial movement before and after surgery
High-protein nutrition in the post-surgical period to support wound healing
Adequate hydration to maintain saliva flow and lip moisture
avoid: Hard, sharp-edged foods that may traumatise the surgically repaired lip in the first 4–6 weeks post-surgery
Spicy or acidic foods that irritate healing mucosal wounds
Foods suspected of triggering Melkersson-Rosenthal flares based on individual diary observation

exerciseGuidelines: There is no exercise restriction for macrocheilia itself. After cheiloplasty, avoid vigorous activity or contact sports for 3–4 weeks to protect the lip repair. Perioral stretching exercises prescribed by the speech-language pathologist are encouraged from 4–6 weeks post-surgery to maintain lip mobility and prevent scar contracture.
dailyManagement: Clean the lip area after every meal to prevent maceration and fungal colonisation beneath everted tissue.
Apply prescribed emollient or anti-inflammatory topical preparations consistently as directed.
Attend all post-surgical review appointments, particularly the 6-week wound check and 6-month aesthetic review.
Perform perioral exercises as prescribed to maintain or improve lip mobility.
Contact the treating team if the lip volume increases rapidly, as this may suggest lymphatic cyst infection or a Melkersson-Rosenthal flare requiring prompt treatment.
prognosis: The prognosis for congenital isolated macrocheilia is good: cheiloplasty achieves a functionally and cosmetically satisfactory outcome in over 85% of patients. Lymphangiomatous macrocheilia tends to recur after sclerotherapy or surgery in a proportion of cases (roughly 20–30%), requiring repeat treatment. Granulomatous macrocheilia from Melkersson-Rosenthal syndrome is a lifelong condition managed rather than cured; most patients achieve acceptable lip volume with ongoing treatment, though the condition can be refractory in a minority. Psychosocial outcomes are significantly improved by early treatment, with most children and adolescents integrating well into school and social settings after surgery.
recoveryTimeline: After cheiloplasty, swelling peaks at 48–72 hours and significantly improves within 2 weeks. A liquid and soft diet is maintained for 4–6 weeks. Sutures are usually absorbable and do not require removal. Speech therapy begins 6–8 weeks post-surgery. Final aesthetic assessment is performed at 6–12 months, when scar maturation is complete. Sclerotherapy for lymphangioma shows maximum response at 8–12 weeks after each injection.
complications: Partial recurrence of lip volume — particularly in lymphangiomatous cases — requiring repeat sclerotherapy or revision cheiloplasty.
Scar contracture causing lip tightness or asymmetry after cheiloplasty, managed with scar massage, silicone gel, or revision surgery.
Wound infection after lip reduction surgery, presenting with erythema, purulent discharge, and pain in the first 2 weeks post-operatively.
Temporary sensory changes in the lip after surgery, typically resolving within 3–6 months as local nerve fibres regenerate.
Chronic granulomatous inflammation in Melkersson-Rosenthal syndrome refractory to standard treatments, occasionally requiring systemic immunosuppression.
Psychological impact of persistent or recurrent lip enlargement, warranting formal psychological support alongside medical management.

supportResources.name: Vascular Birthmarks Foundation
supportResources.description: Provides resources and specialist referral guidance for patients with vascular anomalies including lymphangiomatous macrocheilia.

supportResources.name: Children's Craniofacial Association
supportResources.description: Support and educational resources for families of children with craniofacial conditions.

supportResources.name: FACES: The National Craniofacial Association
supportResources.description: Financial assistance, peer networking, and educational materials for patients and families affected by craniofacial anomalies including lip malformations.

sources.title: Yesudian PD. Orofacial granulomatosis. Clin Exp Dermatol, 2012
Waner M, Suen JY. Hemangiomas and Vascular Malformations of the Head and Neck. Wiley-Liss, 1999 (ISSN overview reference)
Poldervaart MT et al. Sirolimus for vascular anomalies in children: a systematic review. Pediatrics, 2018
Online Mendelian Inheritance in Man (OMIM) — Melkersson-Rosenthal Syndrome (entry 155900)
WHO International Classification of Diseases, 11th Revision — Macrocheilia Q18.6
Alqahtani A et al. 25 years' experience with OK-432 sclerotherapy of lymphatic malformations. J Pediatr Surg, 2012
Bhatt M, Bhatt DL. Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome. StatPearls, NCBI Bookshelf

confusedWithConditions.name: Microcheilia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Microcheilia is abnormally thin or small lips — the opposite defect; both are congenital lip volume anomalies but differ completely in aetiology, appearance, and treatment approach.

confusedWithConditions.name: Macroglossia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Macroglossia is enlargement of the tongue, not the lips; although both are oral soft-tissue overgrowth conditions and can co-occur in Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome, they are anatomically distinct.

confusedWithConditions.name: Other congenital malformations of lips
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: The broader ICD category Q18.0/Q38.0 includes lip pits, double lip, and commissural anomalies; macrocheilia specifically describes abnormal increase in lip volume rather than shape or position anomalies.

relatedConditions.name: Microcheilia
relatedConditions.relevance: The converse congenital lip anomaly — abnormally small or thin lips — managed by the same craniofacial specialists using related surgical techniques.

relatedConditions.name: Macroglossia
relatedConditions.relevance: Tongue enlargement frequently co-occurs with macrocheilia in syndromes such as Beckwith-Wiedemann; combined oral soft-tissue overgrowth magnifies feeding and speech impact.

relatedConditions.name: Other congenital malformations of lips
relatedConditions.relevance: The parent ICD category encompassing lip pits, double lip, and commissural anomalies that share clinical overlap with macrocheilia.

relatedConditions.name: Macrostomia
relatedConditions.relevance: A related congenital oral anomaly involving horizontal widening of the commissure, managed by the same multidisciplinary craniofacial team.

faqs.question: What is macrocheilia?
faqs.answer: Macrocheilia is a congenital condition in which one or both lips are abnormally large due to excess tissue. The enlargement may involve connective tissue, lymphatic channels, or granulomatous inflammation, and can be present from birth or develop progressively.

faqs.question: What causes enlarged lips from birth?
faqs.answer: Congenital macrocheilia most often results from abnormal lymphatic channel development (lymphangioma), excess connective tissue, or — rarely — a genetic overgrowth syndrome such as Beckwith-Wiedemann. These are structural tissue differences present from birth, distinct from allergic swelling.

faqs.question: Is macrocheilia the same as lip filler swelling?
faqs.answer: No. Macrocheilia is a congenital or pathological enlargement of lip tissue from structural overgrowth or infiltration, not an injectable treatment. It is a medical condition distinct from cosmetic lip augmentation.

faqs.question: How is macrocheilia treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment depends on the cause. Lymphangiomatous macrocheilia is often first treated with sclerotherapy injections; surgical lip reduction (cheiloplasty) is used for significant volume or functional problems. Granulomatous macrocheilia from Melkersson-Rosenthal syndrome is treated with intralesional corticosteroids and anti-inflammatory medications.

faqs.question: What is Melkersson-Rosenthal syndrome?
faqs.answer: Melkersson-Rosenthal syndrome is a rare condition characterised by recurring facial swelling (often the lips), a furrowed tongue, and recurring facial palsy. Granulomatous inflammation of the lip causes persistent enlargement over repeated episodes. It typically begins in adolescence or early adulthood.

faqs.question: Does macrocheilia affect speech?
faqs.answer: Yes. Excess lip tissue impairs the lip seal required for bilabial sounds (b, p, m) and can cause a muffled quality to these phonemes. Most children achieve normal articulation after lip reduction surgery and speech therapy.

faqs.question: How is macrocheilia different from angioedema?
faqs.answer: Angioedema causes acute, episodic lip swelling that appears rapidly (over minutes to hours) and fully resolves between attacks, often with urticaria or a trigger. Macrocheilia is persistent, structural lip enlargement that does not come and go. Blood tests (C1 esterase inhibitor levels) distinguish hereditary angioedema.

faqs.question: What is a lymphangioma of the lip?
faqs.answer: A lymphangioma is a congenital malformation of lymphatic channels that produces a soft, cystic swelling in the lip submucosa. It distends the lip from birth and may enlarge gradually. Sclerotherapy with OK-432 or bleomycin is the preferred initial treatment, achieving good volume reduction in 70–80% of cases.

faqs.question: What is cheiloplasty?
faqs.answer: Cheiloplasty is surgical reshaping or reduction of the lips. For macrocheilia, it involves removing excess mucosal and submucosal tissue through intra-oral incisions to reduce lip volume and restore a natural contour. It is performed under general anaesthesia and takes 1–2 hours.

faqs.question: Can macrocheilia recur after treatment?
faqs.answer: Yes, particularly in lymphangiomatous cases, where lymphatic channels can re-expand after sclerotherapy or surgery in roughly 20–30% of patients, requiring repeat treatment. Granulomatous macrocheilia from Melkersson-Rosenthal syndrome is a lifelong condition that is managed rather than permanently cured.

faqs.question: When should I seek urgent care for lip swelling?
faqs.answer: Seek emergency care if lip swelling is sudden, rapid (occurring within minutes), is accompanied by throat tightness, difficulty breathing, or hives. This pattern suggests anaphylaxis or hereditary angioedema — both are emergencies requiring immediate treatment with epinephrine or C1 esterase inhibitor concentrate.

faqs.question: Does macrocheilia affect feeding in infants?
faqs.answer: Yes. Bulky lip tissue interferes with the oral seal needed for effective breastfeeding and bottle feeding, causing milk spillage and extended feed times. A speech-language pathologist or lactation consultant can advise on positioning and specialist teats before surgical correction.

faqs.question: Is macrocheilia associated with any genetic syndromes?
faqs.answer: Yes. Macrocheilia can occur in Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome, Melkersson-Rosenthal syndrome, and vascular malformation syndromes. A clinical geneticist evaluates for these when macrocheilia is accompanied by other anomalies or systemic features.

faqs.question: What specialist treats macrocheilia?
faqs.answer: A craniofacial or maxillofacial plastic surgeon performs lip reduction. An interventional radiologist manages sclerotherapy for lymphangioma. Melkersson-Rosenthal syndrome is managed by a dermatologist or oral medicine specialist. A clinical geneticist coordinates syndromic cases.

faqs.question: Will the lips look normal after cheiloplasty?
faqs.answer: Most patients achieve a natural-looking lip contour and symmetry after cheiloplasty. Scars are placed inside the mouth and are not visible externally. Scar maturation takes 6–12 months; over 85% of patients are satisfied with the cosmetic outcome at long-term follow-up.

faqs.question: Is sirolimus used for macrocheilia?
faqs.answer: Sirolimus, an mTOR inhibitor, is used in refractory lymphangiomatous or combined lymphatic-vascular malformations of the lip that have not responded to sclerotherapy and surgery. It is taken orally and has shown volume reduction in case series, though it requires careful monitoring.

faqs.question: Can macrocheilia be detected before birth?
faqs.answer: Significant macrocheilia from a large lymphangioma or syndromic overgrowth may be visible on a detailed 20-week prenatal ultrasound or fetal MRI. Isolated mild macrocheilia is usually too subtle to detect before birth.

faqs.question: What is a double lip deformity?
faqs.answer: A double lip is a fold of excess mucosa on the inner surface of the upper lip that becomes visible as an apparent second lip when the mouth is open or smiling. It is a congenital variant related to macrocheilia and is corrected by excision of the accessory mucosal fold under local or general anaesthesia.

faqs.question: Does macrocheilia run in families?
faqs.answer: Isolated macrocheilia is usually sporadic. When it occurs as part of a syndrome with known inheritance (autosomal dominant Melkersson-Rosenthal or Beckwith-Wiedemann), recurrence risk depends on the specific genetic mechanism. Genetic counselling clarifies family-specific risk.

faqs.question: How long does recovery take after lip reduction surgery?
faqs.answer: Swelling peaks at 48–72 hours and improves markedly within 2 weeks. A soft diet is maintained for 4–6 weeks. Speech therapy begins 6–8 weeks post-surgery. Scar maturation and the final aesthetic result are assessed at 6–12 months after cheiloplasty.

metaDescription: Macrocheilia is a congenital enlarged lip condition from excess tissue or lymphatic malformation. Learn about cheiloplasty, sclerotherapy, and outcomes. Doctor-reviewed.
